coredata.dc:title: Multilevel resistive switching memristor based on silk fibroin/graphene oxide with image reconstruction functionality
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Shubin
Cheng, Yu
Han, Fang
Fan, Suna
Zhang, Yaopeng
coredata.dc:description: Multilevel storage memristors have great potential for use in neuromorphic computing, high-density storage, and the creation of artificial synapses. In this work, we report a multilevel storage memristor based on graphene oxide (GO)/silk fibroin (SF)/GO memristive layer structure. This memristor incorporates binary and ternary switching behaviors in a single device. It was found that the transition of the behavior of the memristor can be transformed between the two resistive switching modes by regulating the compliance current (Icc ) applied to the device. Both switching behaviors are stable, repeatable, and nonvolatile. Furthermore, the device shows great potential in simulating synaptic plasticity and is applicable to use in artificial neural networks for digital image recognition as well as image compression and reconstruction. The highest accuracy of recognition of handwritten digital images based on the ternary neural network built by the device is as high as 92.3%. This work highlights GO/SF/GO memristors as promising devices for improving the storage density of memory cells and simplifying the structure of the memristor-based storage system.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the fabrication process of the memristor and characterization of its functional layer. (a) Schematic of the preparation process of the GO/SF/GO-based memristor. (b) A photograph of the GO/SF/GO memristor. (c) Atomic force microscopy (AFM) image of the GO/SF/GO film. (d) Results of the XPS analysis of the GO/SF/GO and SF films. (e) FTIR spectra of the GO/SF/GO and SF films. (f) Thickness analysis of the GO/SF/GO film: (i) bottom GO film, (ii) SF/GO film, (iii) GO/SF/GO film, and (iv) a bar chart of the thickness of each layer of the GO/SF/GO film.

Resistive switching characteristics of the GO/SF/GO memristor for Icc > 0.01 A. (a) Typical I–V curves of the GO/SF/GO memristor. (b) Distribution of VSET , VRESET-1 , and VRESET-2 of the GO/SF/GO memristor during 50 switching cycles. (c) Repeatability performance of the memristor. (d-e) Cumulative probability of resistance: (d) HRS and LRS, (e) IRS and LRS. (f) ON/OFF current ratio for different devices as a function of the applied voltage. (g) Time retention characteristic of a device under a reading bias voltage of 0.2 V at room temperature. (h) Stability of the memristor after storage for 100 days. (i) Distribution of VSET , VRESET-1 , and VRESET-2 of GO/SF/GO memristor during 30 switching cycles after storage for 100 days.

Synaptic plasticity of the GO/SF/GO memristor for Icc > 0.01 A. (a–b) The synaptic learning behaviors of the memristor under continuous modulation in DC mode. (c) The synaptic potentiation and depression behavior controlled under PC mode. (d) The paired pulse facilitation (PPF) of the device. (e) Variation of PPF index with the interval time between two continuous voltage pulses (0.05 s width and 2 V amplitude). (f) Long-term potentiation (LTP) under continuous voltage pulses.

Resistive switching characteristics of the GO/SF/GO memristor. (a) Typical I–V characteristics of the GO/SF/GO memristor for various values of Icc ranging from 0.5 mA to 0.01 A. (b) Repeatability of the resistive switching process of the device for Icc = 0.01 A. (c) RS values as a function of the number of cycles for Icc = 0.01 A. (d) Stability of the device in dual-resistance mode at room temperature and a reading bias voltage of 0.2 V for Icc = 0.01 A. (e) Distributions of VSET and VRESET for Icc = 0.01 A. (f) Durability of the memristor in the dual-resistance mode after storage for 100 days for Icc = 0.01 A.

Resistive switching mechanism of the GO/SF/GO memristor. (a) Temperature dependence of resistance. (b) Conductance (G) of the memristor as a function of temperature (T). (c) I–V curves (on a log–log scale) for Icc ≤ 0.01 A of the SET process and fitted curve. (d) Analysis of the I–V characteristics of the RESET process and related fitted curves for Icc > 0.01 A: (i) log–log plot of the LRS region, (ii, iii) value of ln(I/V) as a function of V1/2 in the (ii) IRS and (iii) HRS regions. (e) C–V sweeps of the GO film. (f) UV–vis absorption spectra of the GO film. (g) Energy band diagram of the GO/SF/GO memristor. (h) Conductive AFM current images of the memristor for different RSs for Icc > 0.01 A.
Schematic of the resistive switching mechanism of the GO/SF/GO memristor. (a) Icc ≤ 0.01 A and (b) Icc > 0.01 A.

Simulation of the multilevel storage memristor in artificial quantized neural networks. (a) Similarity between biological synapses and the memristor considered here. (b) Schematic of the trained three-layer fully connected neural network (784 × 30 × 10) used here to recognize handwritten digits obtained from the MNIST database. (c) Recognition accuracies of the ordinary neural network and the neural network utilizing binary and ternary memristors. (d) Three-layer back propagation (BP) neural network (16 × 8 × 16) used to compress and reconstruct images. (e) Result of image processing using the BP neural network: (i) input image, (ii) reconstructed image obtained using the control group, (iii) reconstructed image obtained using the TWN, and (iv) reconstructed image obtained using the BNN.
Comparison of memristive performance of GO/SF/GO based memristor and other different memristors.

head.ce:title: Multilevel resistive switching memristor based on silk fibroin/graphene oxide with image reconstruction functionality

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Multilevel storage memristors have great potential for use in neuromorphic computing, high-density storage, and the creation of artificial synapses. In this work, we report a multilevel storage memristor based on graphene oxide (GO)/silk fibroin (SF)/GO memristive layer structure. This memristor incorporates binary and ternary switching behaviors in a single device. It was found that the transition of the behavior of the memristor can be transformed between the two resistive switching modes by regulating the compliance current (Icc ) applied to the device. Both switching behaviors are stable, repeatable, and nonvolatile. Furthermore, the device shows great potential in simulating synaptic plasticity and is applicable to use in artificial neural networks for digital image recognition as well as image compression and reconstruction. The highest accuracy of recognition of handwritten digital images based on the ternary neural network built by the device is as high as 92.3%. This work highlights GO/SF/GO memristors as promising devices for improving the storage density of memory cells and simplifying the structure of the memristor-based storage system.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Neuromorphic computing has been intensively studied in emerging fields, such as artificial intelligence and high-density storage, because it has the capacity to overcome the bottlenecks of von Neumann computer architectures [1–3]. To utilize neuromorphic computing techniques, it is necessary to develop devices that can simultaneously process and store data. The memristor, which can switch and maintain a high-resistance state (HRS) or a low-resistance state (LRS), is well suited to the construction of neuromorphic computing devices as its structure and working mechanism are similar to those found in the synapses of the human brain [4–6]. During the associated resistive switching process, the HRS and LRS serve as the “1” and “0” of binary code for information storage. However, most currently available dual-resistance state memristors cannot meet the requirements of high-density storage for neuromorphic computing. One effective method to resolve this problem is to exploit new memristors with multilevel resistive states (RSs) [7].

There are two primary methods for achieving multilevel resistive switching. One is to continuously vary the compliance current (Icc ) in different switching cycles, and the other is to achieve multilevel RSs directly in the same resistive switching cycle. In recent literatures, the former has become the more common method. Different RSs can act as different codes in information storage applications, thus endowing the device with multilevel storage performance. For example, Wen et al. [8] prepared a multilevel storage memristor with a long retention time, excellent durability, and repeatability, through using graphene oxide (GO)/silkworm hemolymph (SH) /GO as the memristive layer. This device was able to realize six different RSs via the manipulation of the Icc . As a result, one memristor cell can yield high-density storage. The power consumption of this type of multilevel resistive switching should be decreased in the future since the continuous adjusting of the Icc . In addition, the operating speed is also limited due to the multiple “write” and “read” processes. By contrast, a memristor that achieves multilevel resistive switching in one switching cycle is likely to achieve higher-density storage. For instance, Li et al. [9] successfully fabricated a tristable memristor device using GO@2-(4-tert-butylphenyl)-5-(4-biphenyl)-1,3,4-oxadiazole (PBD)/polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA)/GO@PBD as the memristive layer. This device has three stable RSs in a resistive switching cycle, making multibit storage possible. However, in order to produce an environmentally friendly, biocompatible memristor, biomaterial-based memristive layers may be a better alternative. Silk fibroin (SF), a natural biomaterial obtained from silkworm cocoons, not only exhibits good memristive performance but also possesses high flexibility, excellent biocompatibility, biodegradability, and is ultralightweight. In addition, it is possible to fabricate SF-based memristors with multilevel RSs in one resistive switching via the careful design of the structure of the memristive layer [10,11]. For example, Rabinal et al. [12] reported an SF-based memristor with an ITO/SF/CdSe quantum dots/Al structure. This memristor successfully exhibits multistep resistive switching behavior over 50 stable cycles, while the retention time of the device exceeds 104 s. However, the small switching current ratio between the different RSs of the memristor affects the accuracy of the signal storage. In addition, the preparation of CdSe quantum dots involves a complex multistep process and strict dimensional requirements. For practical use, it is necessary to simplify the preparation process and improve the multilevel switching performance in one switching cycle of memristors.

The development of a memristor with the capacity for multilevel storage in a single switching cycle and binary storage via regulation of the applied condition would represent an important research achievement and yield a device of practical importance. As we all known, GO is a hydrophilic layered material with a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface and edge [8,13], which is beneficial to the formation of interface interaction between GO and SF. Therefore, in this work, we design and fabricate the memristive functional layer with a GO/SF/GO three-layer structure to realize the above goal [14]. The obtained memristor exhibits a trilevel RS in one switching cycle in the case of Icc >0.01 A, and a bilevel RS in the case of Icc less than or equal to 0.01 A. Thus, the developed device permits a single device to be used for both traditional binary storage as well as ternary storage. In addition, our device displays the ability to simulate the short-term and long-term synaptic plasticity of synapses. Based on the results, we perform artificial neural network (ANN) simulations and show that the memristor can be used for high-precision recognition of handwritten digits and high-quality compression and reconstruction of images. Our work demonstrates that GO/SF/GO-based memristors have great potential for multibit data storage, artificial synapse, and neuromorphic computing.

Cocoons of Bombyx mori were purchased from Tongxiang, China. The GO suspension was purchased from Hangzhou Gaoxi Technology Co., Ltd. (Hangzhou, China). Pure Al (99.9999%) was purchased from Zhongnuo Advanced Material Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

The SF solution was prepared in accordance with a method previously reported in the literature [15]. Firstly, the peeled cocoons were boiled twice in a 0.5 wt% Na2CO3 aqueous solution to obtain degummed silk. The degummed silk was then washed with deionized water prior to being dissolved in a 9.0 M lithium bromide (LiBr) aqueous solution. It was subsequently subjected to centrifugation, filtration, and dialysis. Finally, the SF aqueous solution was concentrated to 2–3 wt% at 4 °C.

The dispersed GO suspension was first spin-coated on an ITO-coated glass substrate at 600 rpm for 30 s, at 1500 rpm for 30 s, and at 2000 rpm for 10 s, successively, to obtain GO–ITO films. The SF solution was then spin-coated on the GO–ITO membrane at 3000 rpm for 60 s and 600 rpm for 10 s, successively, to prepare an SF/GO–ITO film. This film was then placed in an 85 vol% ethanol aqueous solution for 1 h to induce a structural transition of SF prior to air drying for 30 min. The GO suspension was then spin-coated onto the SF/GO–ITO film at the same conditions as those used for preparing the first GO layer in order to obtain a GO/SF/GO–ITO film. Finally, the Al was vacuum-evaporated onto the GO/SF/GO composite film to serve as the top electrode.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the GO suspension and the GO/SF/GO film
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the GO suspension (1 mg mL−1) dropped on a carbon film-coated copper grid was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEM2100, JEOL) at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The contents of C, N, and O of the composite films in different depths etching of the memristor were in situ etched and layer-by-layer characterized via X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Axis Supra, Shimadzu). The contents of C, N, and H in the composite films were quantitatively characterized via an element content analyzer (Unicube, Elementar). The thickness of the GO/SF/GO films was measured using a step profiler (Dektak XT, Bruker). The surface morphology and roughness of the composite film were characterized via atomic force microscope (AFM) (SPM-9700HT, Shimadzu). The molecular structure of the GO/SF/GO composite films was characterized via Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy (Nicolet 8700, Thermo Fisher). The C–V curve was measured via an electrochemical workstation (PGSTAT 204, Autolab). The UV–vis spectrum of the GO was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu).

The memristive properties of the memristors were measured using a Keithley 4200A-SCS semiconductor parameter analyzer at a temperature of 25 °C and atmospheric pressure. The local switching properties of the memristor were characterized via the use of conductive AFM.

A schematic of the fabrication process of the memristor is shown in Fig. 1 a. The functional layer of the device is prepared by layering spin-coating, and the SF layer is post-treated with ethanol. Finally, the Al top electrode is prepared by vacuum evaporation. The memristor developed here exhibits a high level of transparency (Fig. 1b), which is attributed to the smooth and thin memristive functional layer. The fact that the functional layer of the memristor is both smooth and flat is of great importance to the stability and uniformity of the memristive performance. In order to ensure that a uniform distribution of GO was achieved on the surface of the indium tin oxide (ITO) and SF film, GO sheets with a small lateral size (400 nm) were selected (Fig. S1). As a result, the GO/SF/GO film had a smooth surface, and the root mean square (RMS) of its surface roughness was 7.843 nm (Fig. 1c). And as shown in Fig. 1f, the thicknesses of the layers that make up the GO/SF/GO film are approximately 20, 38, and 21 nm, respectively, from bottom to top.

The XPS and element content analysis results indicate that the functional layer is composed of N, C, H, and O (Fig. 1d and S3). As shown in Fig. S2, after post-treatment of the SF film with ethanol, the peak at 1620 cm−1 is sharper than the other peaks, and new peaks located at 1698 and 1266 cm−1 appear. These characteristic peaks are typical of the β-sheet structure, indicating that ethanol induces a conformational transition of SF from helix/random coil to β-sheet [16]. Furthermore, it can be concluded that the introduction of GO on the SF surface do not induce conformational changes in SF. This is indicated by the similarity of the infrared spectra of the two films (Fig. 1e and S2). The above results are likely to have a favorable effect on the resistive switching mechanism of the device, which is discussed in the following mechanism section.

The memristive performance of the memristor was studied in the direct-current (DC) voltage sweep mode. The ITO electrode was grounded, and a potential was applied to the Al electrode. The value of Icc was set to 0.05 or 0.1 A. For Icc > 0.01 A, the I–V characteristic curve exhibits three RSs in one cycle, and can repeat stably for 50 times (Fig. 2 a). This phenomenon is not observed in pristine GO memristor and SF/GO memristor (Fig. S4), indicating that the interface interaction between SF and GO plays great role on the ternary switching behavior. When subject to a negative voltage scan, the device undergoes the SET process at − 3.9 V, which is defined as VSET . When subject to a positive voltage scan, the device has two RESET processes at 2.4 and 4.1 V. These two voltages are defined as VRESET-1 and VRESET-2 , respectively. The resistive switching voltages of the HRS and LRS are predominately distributed in the ranges of − 1–−2 V and 3–4 V. The resistive switching voltage of the intermediate resistance state (IRS), i.e., VRESET-1 , is concentrated in the range of 2–3 V and exhibits higher repeatability than the HRS and LRS (Fig. 2b). It is worth noting that the quiet testing environment and the tight contact between the probe and top electrode are necessary for the stable working voltages. In addition, the above three RSs could be used to represent “0”, “1”, and “2”, respectively. This indicates that the fabricated GO/SF/GO-based memristor could be used to realize ternary operations, resulting in an increase in information storage density. Alternatively, the HRS could be regarded as “OFF”, while the LRS and IRS could be regarded as “ON”. In addition, during the 50 cycles of the device, the resistance of the HRS, IRS, and LRS take values of approximately 106, 104, and 102 Ω, respectively (Fig. 2c–e). At the same time, different memristors display stable and repeatable memristive performance (Fig. S5). We should note that although the GO/SF/GO-based memristor displays relatively good repeatability during the first 50 cycles, the endurance needs to be furtherly improved in the future.

A large ON–OFF current ratio is beneficial to distinguish the three RSs, which leads to higher accuracy in the readability of the stored information represented by the conductance signal. Fig. 2f shows the curves of the ON–OFF current ratio of different GO/SF/GO memristors as a function of voltage. The distribution of the ON–OFF current ratios of the devices in the LRS and IRS are seen to be in the ranges of 103–105 and 101–102, respectively, indicating that the memristive performance of the GO/SF/GO-based memristor is repeatable. In addition, the high ON–OFF current ratio observed here is beneficial to the discrimination between the different RSs and to the accuracy of the information storage process. Under a reading bias voltage of 0.2 V, the three RSs of this memristor were stable for more than 104 s (Fig. 2g). In order to guarantee the practical application, the memristor should possess good durability and environmental stability. As shown in Fig. 2h, the GO/SF/GO memristor still remain stable three-resistance resistive switching for 30 times after storage for 100 days at room temperature. In addition, VSET , VRESET-1 , and VRESET-2 are still distributed at − 1–−2 V, 2–3 V, and 4–5 V (Fig. 2i), respectively, which show little change compared with the freshly fabricated memristor (Fig. 2b). This indicates that the memristor has good nonvolatile performance, stability, and durability.

Under external stimulation, synaptic weight can be adjusted by the release and reception of neurotransmitters. This adjustability of synaptic weight is called synaptic plasticity, which is the basis of memory and learning [17]. The GO/SF/GO-based memristor shows the ability to simulate the plasticity of the synapse. As shown in Fig. 3 a and b, under a continuous bias voltage in DC mode, the memristor exhibits synaptic learning behavior, and the voltage required for the SET and RESET processes of the memristor decreases gradually, which is similar to the learning behavior of synapse and indicates that the device can realize resistive switching with reduced energy consumption after multiple learning. In order to simulate the enhancement and suppression characteristics of synapse in pulse current (PC) mode, the pulse voltages with the same amplitude (5 V) were continuously applied to the device (Fig. 3c). Under the positive pulses, the current increased with the increasing number of pulses, which represents a potentiation process. Subsequently, when the negative pulses were applied, the current decreased with the increase of the number of negative pulses, which represents a depression process. The above potentiation and depression processes have features that are similar to those observed in synaptic plasticity, which is the ability of synapse to change its weight [18].

In addition, the short-term synaptic plasticity and long-term synaptic plasticity of the device were also studied. First, when an excitatory pulse with an amplitude of 2 V and pulse width of 0.05 s was applied to the device, the output current increased, that is, the excitatory post-synaptic current (EPSC) was generated (Fig. S6). This behavior is characterized by short-term potentiation (STP) effect, and is known as single pulse facilitation. Then, when two identical pulses were applied to the device successively, the output current stimulated by the latter pulse was higher than that of the previous one (Fig. 3d). This behavior is similar to the paired pulse facilitation (PPF) feature of synapses, which reflects the short-term synaptic plasticity of the synapse and plays a key role in the realization of advanced learning and memory of the human brain [6,19–21]. In this work, PPF is calculated according to

, where I1 is the current excited by the first pulse, I2 is the current excited by the second pulse, and I2 is greater than I1 . In addition, synaptic plasticity relates to the interval time of continuous pulses [22]. As shown in Fig. S7 and Fig. 3e, the PPF index decreased with the increase of interval time, which was consistent with the characteristic of synaptic behaviors. Finally, the long-term synaptic plasticity characteristic of the device was explored. The current of the device increased continuously under the continuous pulses with an amplitude of 2 V (Fig. 3f). This behavior is called the long-term potentiation (LTP) effect [23]. From the above results, we believe that the memristor developed in this work has the potential to be used in the construction of artificial synapses and brain-inspired devices.

Icc is an important parameter that affects the memristive performance of memristors [24,25]. In order to understand the influence of Icc on the GO/SF/GO memristor developed here, we explored the memristive performance of the memristor for different values of Icc . It was found that the change in Icc can induce a transformation from binary storage to ternary storage in the same device. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the memristor exhibits the conventional dual-resistance states when Icc ≤ 0.01 A. The SET and RESET processes occur under positive and negative voltages, respectively. These results are distinct from those obtained for Icc > 0.01 A (Fig. 2a). It is notable that this is the first SF-based memristor that realizes switching between binary and ternary storages in the same device. In addition, for Icc ≤ 0.01 A, the ON/OFF current ratio and the values of VSET and VRESET increase with the increase of Icc , which suggests that the memristor can realize multilevel RSs in different switching cycles by modulating Icc . The ON/OFF current ratio can reach a maximum of 102, which is much smaller than that observed in the three-resistance resistive switching mode (Fig. S8).

In order to further investigate the memristive performance of the device, we set Icc to 0.01 A. For this value of Icc , the ON/OFF current ratio of the memristor is maximal, and the performance of the device is very stable. From the 100 scanning cycles shown in Fig. 4b, we can find that the device exhibits two stable resistance states, and the resistive switching performance does not decrease significantly. The resistance of the HRSs and LRSs are observed to be close to 105 and 102 Ω, respectively (Fig. 4c). In addition, the retention time of both the HRS and the LRS is found to be as large as 104 s (Fig. 4d), which is superior to that observed in most biomaterial-based memristors [26–28]. The values of VSET and VRESET are observed to be predominantly in the ranges of 2–3 V and − 2–−3 V, respectively (Fig. 4e). When being considered the dual-resistance resistive switching mode of the device, the device is found to maintain stable dual-resistance resistive switching after storage at room temperature for 100 days (Fig. 4f). In addition, the distributions of VSET and VRESET of the memristor after storage for 100 days were still concentrated although the voltage amplitude decreased (Fig. S9). This may be attributed to the conformational transition of SF during the storage. The above results confirmed that the dual-resistance resistive switching mode of the device are environmental stable and durable.

Here, we investigate the mechanism of the memristor for different Icc , which is of great significance for explaining the different resistive switching behaviors of GO/SF/GO memristors. Firstly, we study the temperature dependence of resistance (Fig. 5 a). The resistance of the LRS was found to decrease with increasing temperature, and the relationship between the conductance (G) and the temperature (T) was found to be G = 2.68 × 10−3 T − 2.2 (Fig. 5b). This is in contrast with the typical behavior of the metal, indicating that the main mechanism is not induced by the formation or breakage of metal conductive filaments during the resistive switching process [8,40,41].

The log–log plots of the I–V curves for the two resistive switching modes were analyzed (Fig. 5c and d). For Icc ≤ 0.01 A, the initial slope (region 1) of the HRS during the SET process was found to be approximately equal to 1. This indicates that the current transfer obeys Ohm's law at low voltage. As the voltage increases (region 2), the slope increases to 1.76, which indicates that the electrical properties conform to Child's law. Then, the current is seen to increase sharply (region 3). The above results indicate that the properties of the GO/SF/GO memristor are a result of the space-charge-limited conduction (SCLC) mechanism for Icc ≤ 0.01 A. This suggests that the trapping and releasing of electrons at specific energy levels are responsible for the resistance variation [8,42,43]. We also note that region 4 (Fig. 5c; slope ≈ 1.05) conforms to Ohm's law.

For Icc > 0.01 A, the device exhibits multi-resistance resistive switching. However, the electron transport in the SET process is still dominated by the SCLC mechanism (Fig. S11). During the RESET-1 process, the slope of the I–V curve in the LRS was found to be approximately 0.95 (region 1), following the law of ohmic conduction. In addition, we found that during the two RESET processes (regions 2 and 3), the relationship between current and voltage obeyed the Poole–Frenkel (P–F) emission theory [44]:

are closely fitted by straight lines with slopes of 1.15 and 0.94, respectively (Fig. 5d-ii and 5d-iii).

Since the top electrode (Al) is an active metal, the formation and fracture of conductive filaments may be affected by the transference of aluminum ions. In order to verify this, we characterized the composition of the memristor at different depths after the SET process through in situ XPS layer-by-layer scan with the help of in situ etching (Fig. S12(a)). According to the peak intensity of different elements at different depths, we deduce the position of memristive functional layers (Figs. S12(b–e)). For example, once N 1 s peak appeared and the corresponding intensity increased gradually, the scanned position can be confirmed as the SF layer. From the top electrode to the bottom electrode, the intensity of the Al 2p peaks decreases with the deepening of depth and disappears in the bottom GO layer. This indicates that Al3+ exists and transfers during the SET process, but no Al conductive filaments are formed throughout the memristive functional layer. Thus, ionic conduction of Al3+ is not the main resistive switching mechanism of this device. Compared with the top GO layer, the intensity of the O 1 s peak of the bottom GO layer is much greater, indicating that there is a significant difference of the O content in the bottom and top GO layers. This corresponds to the possible valence change mechanism (VCM) dominated by oxygen ion oxidation and reduction within the device [8,45]. After the SET process, the chemical bonds containing oxygen ions (lattice oxygen) within the functional layer break under the action of an electric field, and the newly released oxygen ions migrate away from their original positions to the ITO electrode, leaving a defect containing oxygen vacancies (non-lattice oxygen) [46]. Oxygen ions gradually deposit on the bottom GO layer as they migrate, so the intensity of the O 1 s peak increases as the depth deepens. This is attributed to the energy band of the functional layer. In order to determine the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy levels of GO, the cyclic voltammetry (C–V) curve and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum of the GO film were measured. As shown in Fig. 5e, GO showed oxidation behavior, and its oxidation peak appeared at 0.88 eV, which is also the C–V onset ionization potential (Eonset ). The EHOMO of GO can be calculated by:

In addition, the ELUMO and Eg of GO were calculated based on the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 5f). Eg of GO was calculated to be 2.71 eV according to the Planck equation [48]:

[49]. Moreover, the LUMO and HOMO values of SF were − 1.67 eV and − 5.18 eV, respectively [12]. The energy band diagram of the GO/SF/GO device is shown in Fig. 5g.

The local resistive switching characteristics of the GO/SF/GO film were characterized via conductive AFM (Fig. 5h). In the initial state (0 V), it was found that there were very few conductive spots. For a bias voltage of − 5 V applied to the tip, the current images clearly indicate localized conductive regions on the 1 × 1 μm2 scanned area. This corresponds to the SET process. Subsequently, when a bias voltage of 2 V was applied in situ to the tip, the local conductive regions gradually disappeared until there were almost no conductive spots. This corresponds to the RESET process. Subsequently, a 5 V voltage was applied to the same area, and the local conduction spots reappeared. This represents the device switching back to the LRS. Here, we hypothesize that the formation and fracture of conductive filaments within the film are the primary reasons for the observed resistive switching behaviors [50].

Based on the above analysis, we hypothesize that for Icc ≤ 0.01 A, the resistive switching is determined by the carrier trapping/de-trapping mechanism (Fig. 6 a). This mechanism is related to the structure of the SF and GO. The primary structure of the SF protein is (-Gly-Ser-Gly-Ala-Gly-Ala-)n [30], and there are an amount of amino (NH2) and carboxyl (COOH). Carboxyl is known to produce oxygen ions and oxygen vacancies. Furthermore, GO contains a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups, which are known to form oxygen ions. Thus, we speculate that the conductive filaments are composed of oxygen vacancy traps [8]. According to the device energy band diagram, when the negative potential is applied to the Al electrode, the electrons transfer from the Al electrode to the LUMO energy level of GO [8]. In the initial state of the device, the number of injected electrons is small, and the electrons are trapped by the GO and SF. The conduction of the material thus obeys Ohm’s law (Fig. 6a-i). With an increasing positive bias voltage, the number of injected electrons increases significantly. Because SF has a high LUMO energy level, there is a potential barrier at the interface between SF and GO, and the majority of the injected electrons are captured by this barrier. In this regime, the electron transfer conforms to Child's law (Fig. 6a-ii). When the voltage is increased further, the electron-filled traps form conductive filaments, which link the upper and lower electrodes. The injected electrons then move freely in the functional layer across these filaments, resulting in a sharp increase in current and the memristor instantly switching to the LRS (Fig. 6a-iii). Finally, when a reverse voltage is applied to the top electrode, the electron-occupied traps release electrons, and the memristor returns to its HRS (Fig. 6a-iv).

For Icc > 0.01 A, the conduction of electrons in the SET process is described by the SCLC mechanism, while the RESET process is dominated by the P–F emission mechanism. When a negative voltage is applied to the top electrode, electrons are injected from the Al electrode and trapped by charge traps containing oxygen vacancies in the top GO and SF layers and the potential barrier between the interfaces (Fig. 6b-ii). When the applied voltage is increased to the value VSET , the memristive functional film is almost completely occupied by electrons. When the conductive filaments connect the upper and lower electrodes, the device switches from HRS to LRS (Fig. 6b-iii). When a reverse voltage is applied to the top electrode, the reverse migration of electrons is induced by the reverse electric field, heat, and the change in the Coulomb barrier of the electrons. Due to the large value of Icc , a large number of electrons are injected into the functional layer from the bottom electrode, resulting in a high internal reverse electric field. In this circumstance, heat is generated within the functional layer subject to the electric field. Due to thermal effects, the charges in the trap and the interface barrier can pass through the barrier, and then be excited into the conduction band of the oxide to transfer, resulting in P–F emission. As a result of the internal electric field, the Coulomb barrier of the electrons was reduced, which leads to an increase in the emission rate of the trapped electrons [51]. Thus, an increased number of electrons in the functional layer are emitted back to the Al electrode, and the previously migrated oxygen ions recombine with oxygen vacancies, leading to the destruction of the conductive filaments and the reduction in the conductive current in the functional layer. It is notable that there is a certain potential barrier between GO and SF, which affects the transmission process of electrons and leads to the conductive filaments do not break instantaneously or completely. The conductive filaments are instead reversibly and gradually narrowed. At the specific applied voltage of VRESET-1 , the electrons trapped in the top GO layer are completely emitted back to the Al electrode, and the device switches from LRS to IRS (Fig. 6b-iv). With the continued increase in the voltage, a large number of electrons in the bottom GO layer are emitted back toward the Al electrode. When the voltage reaches VRESET-2 , the conductive filaments are completely disrupted, and the device switches to its HRS (Fig. 6b-v).

Neuromorphic computing is likely to be an important application of memristors [10,52], and multilevel storage memristors have the advantages of high adaptability, high efficiency, and low power consumption [33]. Here, we present the application potential of GO/SF/GO memristors in neuromorphic computing and storage. Fig. 7 a shows the similarity between the biological synapse and the memristor. The Al electrode and ITO electrode of the memristor correspond to a presynaptic membrane and a postsynaptic membrane, respectively. The memristive functional layer undergoes a process similar to the synaptic cleft, and oxygen ions can be considered similar to neurotransmitters. The nonvolatile performance of memristors can not only simulate the changes in synaptic weights but is also beneficial for building a highly integrated neural network structure. This provides the possibility of ANN integrating learning and memory functionalities [53–55]. In this study, we used the conductance values of the GO/SF/GO memristor to represent the synaptic weight, and quantized/mapped ordinary fully connected neural networks into a binary neural network (BNN) and a ternary weight network (TWN), that is, synaptic weight has only 2 or 3 different values, and present two designs for quantitative ANNs [56]. The three-resistance and dual-resistance memristors correspond to a TWN and a BNN, respectively. During the simulation process, the resistance values of HRS, LRS, and IRS were represented by −1, 1, and a determined value, respectively, which was calculated according to the ratio of resistance values of three different resistive states. In order to provide an accurate comparison of the performance of these two ANNs, we also established a control group network. The control group is an ordinary multilayer perceptron model, which only “symmetrically” limits the range of the network parameter updates during the network training process. The weight update is closely related to the evolution of the conductance of the memristor in the training process of the ANN. During the ANN simulation, the memristive curves shown in Fig. 2g and Fig. 4d were used as realistic behavioral models, the recognition and classification of handwritten digits obtained from the Modified National Institute of Standards and Technology (MNIST) database and the compression and reconstruction of images. In the process of signal transmission, memristors encode information in the RSs. In the construction of cognitive computing neural networks based on memristors, the synaptic weights in the neural networks are stored as conductance states, and these are used to perform relevant computing tasks at appropriate locations [18].

We first constructed a simple three-layer fully connected neural network (784 × 30 × 10) to perform the task of recognizing handwritten digits obtained from the MNIST database (Fig. 7b). After 100 training epochs, we obtained the classification and recognition accuracy of the three neural networks considered here (Fig. 7c). When the control group neural network was mapped to the TWN, the recognition accuracy of the TWN was found to be approximately 92.3%, which is better than that obtained using the BNN (85.2%) and closer to the level of the control group. This indicates that three-value storage yields better performance in the application of neuromorphic computing networks than binary storage.

Here, we also note that the memristor-based ANN can realize matrix vector multiplication calculation in a single step, which significantly reduces the energy consumption of the fully built ANN based on the hardware system. Furthermore, the memristor-based ANN was found to reduce the risk of data loss because of the non-volatile performance of memristors (Fig. 2h). Therefore, the memristor-based ANN has great potential for application in the storage and reading of digital or image data [57–59]. Here, we built a three-layer back propagation (BP) neural network (16 × 8 × 16) (Fig. 7d) to compress and reconstruct images (Fig. 7e-i). Fig. 7e-ii shows the reconstructed image obtained using the control group, while a conventional BP neural network model was selected as the control group. Fig. 7e-iii and 7e-iv show the reconstructed images obtained using the TWN and BNN, respectively. It can be seen that the image quality obtained via the use of the different quantization neural networks differs. The phenomenon of the blocking artifact in the image reconstructed using the TWN (Fig. 7e-iii) is less notable than that observed in the image reconstructed using the BNN (Fig. 7e-iv), indicating that the use of a three-resistance memristor leads to better performance than that obtained using a dual-resistance memristor. The quality of the images obtained using the three-resistance memristor is also seen to be higher than the competing models. The simulation results indicate that the selection of a specific multivalued memristor array may be able to meet the requirements for high-quality image reconstruction.

It was demonstrated that the GO/SF/GO memristor reported in this study can be used to obtain three RSs in a single switching cycle as well as dual-resistance switching. The transition between these two switching modes can be induced via the control of the Icc . This switching behavior is attributed to the interface effect between the GO layer and the SF layer and indicates that the GO/SF/GO memristor can be used both in ternary storage and binary storage. Furthermore, the three RSs are found to exhibit high stability, repeatability, and good nonvolatile performance. The behavior of the dual-resistance switching in the memristor is attributed to the SCLC mechanism, while the three-resistance switching process is regulated by the SCLC and P–F emission mechanisms. In addition, this memristor is able to simulate short-term and long-term synaptic plasticity. The GO/SF/GO memristor-based TWN exhibits a recognition accuracy of 92.3% when analyzing handwritten numbers obtained from the MNIST database and shows good potential for the applications of image compression and reconstruction. The memristor device developed in this work has significant potential for use in both multilevel storage and neural morphological computing.
